title: Democracy and the Constitution
overview: The UK is a parliamentary democracy with a constitutional monarchy. Unlike most countries, the British constitution is not written down in a single document. This lesson covers the development of British democracy, the nature of the UK constitution, the role of the monarchy, and how Parliament and elections work.

study_sections.heading: The Development of British Democracy
study_sections.content:
Democracy is a system of government where the whole adult population gets a say - either by direct voting or by choosing representatives to make decisions on their behalf.

At the turn of the 19th century, Britain was not a democracy as we know it today. Only men over 21 who owned a certain amount of property could vote. Over the course of the 19th century, the number of people with the right to vote (the franchise) grew.

In the 1830s and 1840s, a group called the Chartists campaigned for six reforms:
• Every man to have the vote
• Elections every year
• All regions to be equal in the electoral system
• Secret ballots
• Any man to be able to stand as an MP
• MPs to be paid

At the time, the campaign was seen as a failure. However, by 1918 most of these reforms had been adopted. In 1918, voting was extended to women over 30. In 1928, the vote was extended to all men and women over 21. In 1969, the voting age was reduced to 18.

study_sections.heading: The British Constitution
study_sections.content:
A constitution is a set of principles by which a country is governed, including all the institutions responsible for running the country and how their power is kept in check.

The British constitution is not written down in any single document - it is described as 'unwritten'. This is mainly because the UK has never had a revolution which led to a totally new system of government. Most other major democracies (like the USA and France) have written constitutions.

The main parts of government are:
• The monarchy
• Parliament (House of Commons and House of Lords)
• The Prime Minister
• The cabinet
• The judiciary (courts)
• The police
• The civil service
• Local government

In addition, there are devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

study_sections.heading: The Monarchy
study_sections.content:
The monarch is the head of state of the UK. The UK has a constitutional monarchy - the king or queen does not rule the country but appoints the government which the people have chosen in a democratic election.

The monarch invites the leader of the party with the largest number of MPs to become the Prime Minister. The monarch has regular meetings with the Prime Minister and can advise, warn and encourage, but decisions on government policies are made by the PM and cabinet.

King Charles III became sovereign in 2022 following the death of Queen Elizabeth II on 8 September 2022. Queen Elizabeth II had reigned since 1952 and celebrated her Diamond Jubilee (60 years) in 2012. His heir apparent is William, Prince of Wales. William's children are George, Charlotte and Louis.

The monarch has important ceremonial roles including the opening of the new parliamentary session each year, when the monarch makes a speech summarising the government's policies. All Acts of Parliament are made in the monarch's name.

The National Anthem is 'God Save the King (or Queen)'. New citizens swear or affirm loyalty to the King as part of the citizenship ceremony.

study_sections.heading: The House of Commons
study_sections.content:
The House of Commons is regarded as the more important of the two chambers in Parliament because its members are democratically elected. Each MP represents a parliamentary constituency.

MPs have a number of responsibilities:
• Represent everyone in their constituency
• Help to create new laws
• Scrutinise and comment on what the government is doing
• Debate important national issues

The Prime Minister and almost all cabinet members are MPs. Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker. The Speaker is neutral, does not represent a political party, and is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot.

study_sections.heading: The House of Lords
study_sections.content:
Members of the House of Lords (called peers) are not elected and do not represent a constituency. Until 1958, all peers were either hereditary (inherited their title), senior judges, or Church of England bishops.

Since 1958, the Prime Minister has had the power to nominate life peers - people appointed just for their own lifetime. Life peers are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister.

Since 1999, hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords - they now elect a few of their number to represent them.

The House of Lords is normally more independent of the government. It can suggest amendments or propose new laws, and checks laws passed by the House of Commons. The House of Commons has powers to overrule the House of Lords.

study_sections.heading: Elections
study_sections.content:
MPs are elected at a General Election, which is held at least every five years. If an MP dies or resigns, there is a byelection in their constituency.

MPs are elected through 'first past the post' - in each constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected. The government is usually formed by the party that wins the majority of constituencies.

The present voting age of 18 was set in 1969. Adult citizens of the UK, and citizens of the Commonwealth and the Irish Republic who are resident in the UK, can vote in all public elections.

To vote, you must be on the electoral register. Polling stations are open from 7.00 am until 10.00 pm on election day. People vote by filling in a ballot paper in a private polling booth. If it is difficult to get to a polling station, you can register for a postal ballot.

key_facts: Democracy means the whole adult population gets a say in government.
In the early 19th century, only men over 21 who owned property could vote.
The Chartists campaigned for voting reform in the 1830s and 1840s.
The six Chartist demands included votes for every man, secret ballots and payment for MPs.
By 1918, most Chartist reforms had been adopted.
In 1918, women over 30 were given the vote.
In 1928, all men and women over 21 were given the vote.
In 1969, the voting age was reduced to 18.
The British constitution is not written down in a single document.
The constitution is described as 'unwritten'.
The UK is a constitutional monarchy.
The monarch appoints the government but does not rule the country.
King Charles III became sovereign in 2022.
Queen Elizabeth II died on 8 September 2022.
Queen Elizabeth II reigned from 1952 and celebrated her Diamond Jubilee in 2012.
The heir apparent to King Charles III is William, Prince of Wales.
All Acts of Parliament are made in the monarch's name.
The National Anthem is 'God Save the King (or Queen)'.
The House of Commons is the more important of the two chambers.
MPs are elected and represent constituencies.
The Speaker chairs debates in the House of Commons and is politically neutral.
The Speaker is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot.
Members of the House of Lords are called peers.
Life peers have been appointed since 1958.
Since 1999, hereditary peers lost the automatic right to attend the Lords.
General Elections are held at least every five years.
If an MP dies or resigns, there is a byelection.
The voting system for MPs is 'first past the post'.
Polling stations are open from 7.00 am until 10.00 pm on election day.
UK citizens and Commonwealth/Irish Republic citizens can vote in all public elections.
memory_hook: Remember voting rights timeline: 1918 (women over 30), 1928 (all adults over 21), 1969 (reduced to 18). Chartists' 6 demands: 'Every Vote Secret - Any MP Paid' (Every man votes, yearly elections, equal regions, Secret ballot, Any man MP, Paid MPs). Speaker = neutral, chosen by secret ballot.

questions.prompt: When was the voting age reduced to 18?
questions.explanation: In 1969, the voting age was reduced to 18 for men and women.

questions.prompt: When were women first given the vote?
questions.explanation: In 1918, women over the age of 30 were given voting rights.

questions.prompt: When were all adults over 21 given the vote?
questions.explanation: In 1928, men and women were given the right to vote at age 21.

questions.prompt: What were the Chartists?
options: Campaigners for voting reform in the 1830s and 1840s
questions.explanation: The Chartists campaigned for six democratic reforms in the 1830s and 1840s.

questions.prompt: Is the UK constitution written down?
options: Yes, it was written after the Civil War
Yes, in the Human Rights Act
questions.explanation: The British constitution is not written down in any single document, so it is described as 'unwritten'.

questions.prompt: What type of monarchy does the UK have?
questions.explanation: The UK has a constitutional monarchy - the monarch appoints the government but does not rule the country.

questions.prompt: When did King Charles III become sovereign?
questions.explanation: King Charles III became sovereign in 2022 following the death of Queen Elizabeth II.

questions.prompt: When did Queen Elizabeth II die?
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth II died on 8 September 2022.

questions.prompt: How long did Queen Elizabeth II reign?
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth II reigned since her father's death in 1952 and celebrated her Diamond Jubilee (60 years) in 2012.

questions.prompt: Who is the heir apparent to King Charles III?
questions.explanation: The heir apparent to King Charles III is his elder son, William, Prince of Wales.

questions.prompt: Which chamber of Parliament is more important?
questions.explanation: The House of Commons is regarded as the more important chamber because its members are democratically elected.

questions.prompt: Who chairs debates in the House of Commons?
questions.explanation: Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker.

questions.prompt: Is the Speaker a member of a political party?
options: Yes, they are from the governing party
Yes, they are from any party
questions.explanation: The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though they are an MP.

questions.prompt: How is the Speaker chosen?
options: By other MPs in a secret ballot
questions.explanation: The Speaker is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot.

questions.prompt: What are members of the House of Lords called?
questions.explanation: Members of the House of Lords are known as peers.

questions.prompt: Since when can the PM nominate life peers?
questions.explanation: Since 1958, the Prime Minister has had the power to nominate life peers.

questions.prompt: What happened to hereditary peers in 1999?
options: Lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords
questions.explanation: Since 1999, hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords.

questions.prompt: How often must a General Election be held?
questions.explanation: MPs are elected at a General Election, which is held at least every five years.

questions.prompt: What is a byelection?
options: An election held when an MP dies or resigns
questions.explanation: If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election called a byelection in that constituency.

questions.prompt: What is the UK's voting system for MPs?
questions.explanation: MPs are elected through 'first past the post' - the candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins.

questions.prompt: What hours are polling stations open?
questions.explanation: On election day, the polling station will be open from 7.00 am until 10.00 pm.

questions.prompt: Who can vote in all public elections in the UK?
options: UK citizens, and Commonwealth/Irish Republic citizens resident in the UK
All EU citizens resident in the UK
Anyone over 18 living in the UK
questions.explanation: Adult citizens of the UK, and citizens of the Commonwealth and the Irish Republic resident in the UK, can vote in all public elections.

flashcards: Chairs debates, neutral, chosen by secret ballot
Election when an MP dies or resigns
Who can vote in all elections?
UK citizens and Commonwealth/Irish Republic citizens resident in UK

supportive_messages.high_score: Excellent! You understand British democracy and the constitution brilliantly! 🗳️
supportive_messages.medium_score: Good work! Focus on the voting rights timeline and how Parliament works! 📚
supportive_messages.low_score: Keep going - understanding democracy is crucial for the test! 💪
